content.content:
1	# SuperMac Go Rewrite Design
2	
3	**Status**: Approved (revised after spec review)
4	**Date**: 2026-04-08
5	**Author**: GAB (CosmoLabs)
6	**Scope**: Full CLI rewrite from Bash to Go
7	**Review**: 4 critical, 7 medium, 6 low issues found — critical addressed below
8	
9	## Overview
10	
11	Rewrite SuperMac from Bash to Go. 1:1 port of all 10 modules maintaining the
12	same CLI interface (`mac <category> <action>`). Go-native improvements:
13	`--json` output, auto-generated shell completions, self-update, real config
14	system. The Go CLI becomes the engine for a future desktop application.
15	
16	## Architecture
17	
18	### Approach: Cobra + Package-per-Module
19	
20	Cobra for CLI backbone (subcommands, help, completions, man pages). Each
21	module is an isolated Go package implementing a shared interface. Platform
22	calls (osascript, defaults, airport) centralized in a `platform` package.
23	
24	### Project Structure
25	
26	```
27	supermac/
28	  cmd/
29	    mac/main.go              Entrypoint (wires Cobra root command)
30	  internal/
31	    module/
32	      module.go              Module interface: Name, Commands, Help
33	      registry.go            Auto-discovers and registers modules
34	    modules/
35	      wifi/wifi.go           Implements module.Interface
36	      system/system.go
37	      network/network.go
38	      display/display.go
39	      dock/dock.go
40	      finder/finder.go
41	      dev/dev.go
42	      audio/audio.go
43	      screenshot/screenshot.go
44	    config/
45	      config.go              Loads ~/.supermac/config.yaml
46	    output/
47	      output.go              Colored, JSON, quiet formatters
48	      colors.go              ANSI color helpers
49	    update/
50	      update.go              Check GitHub Releases, prompt/apply
51	    platform/
52	      airport.go             macOS WiFi binary wrapper
53	      defaults.go            defaults read/write wrappers
54	      osascript.go           AppleScript bridge
55	    version/
56	      version.go             Single source of truth (stamped via ldflags)
57	  pkg/
58	    supermac/                Public API (for desktop app later)
59	  completions/               Generated shell completion scripts
60	  config.example.yaml        Sample config with comments
61	  Makefile                   build, install, test, lint, release
62	  go.mod
63	  go.sum
64	```
65	
66	Key boundary: `internal/` is private to the CLI (can change freely). `pkg/`
67	is the stable public API the desktop app will consume.
68	
69	## Module Interface
70	
71	```go
72	// internal/module/module.go
73	
74	type Module interface {
75	    Name() string
76	    ShortDescription() string
77	    Emoji() string
78	    Commands() []Command
79	    Search(term string) []SearchResult  // Full-text search across module commands
80	}
81	
82	type Command struct {
83	    Name        string
84	    Description string
85	    Aliases     []string
86	    Args        []Arg
87	    Flags       []Flag                  // Per-command flags (e.g., --sort, --force)
88	    Run         func(ctx *Context) error
89	}
90	
91	type Flag struct {
92	    Name         string
93	    Shorthand    string                 // Single char, e.g., "s" for --sort
94	    Description  string
95	    DefaultValue string
96	    Required     bool
97	}
98	
99	type Arg struct {
100	    Name        string
101	    Required    bool
102	    Description string
103	}
104	
105	type Context struct {
106	    Config   *config.Config
107	    Output   output.Writer
108	    Platform platform.Interface       // Injected — real or mock
109	    Prompt   PromptInterface          // Interactive confirmation (mockable)
110	    Args     []string
111	    Flags    map[string]string        // Parsed flag values
112	    Verbose  bool
113	    DryRun   bool
114	}
115	
116	type SearchResult struct {
117	    Command     string
118	    Description string
119	    Module      string
120	}
121	```
122	
123	### Interactive Prompts
124	
125	```go
126	// internal/module/prompt.go
127	type PromptInterface interface {
128	    Confirm(msg string) (bool, error)       // Y/n dialog
129	    Input(msg string) (string, error)       // Free text input
130	    Select(msg string, opts []string) (int, error) // Choice selection
131	}
132	```
133	
134	Destructive operations (`system cleanup`, `network reset`, `dock reset`) use
135	`ctx.Prompt.Confirm()` which can be mocked in tests. `--yes` flag sets a
136	non-interactive Prompt implementation that always returns true.
137	
138	### Error Handling
139	
140	```go
141	// internal/module/errors.go
142	type ExitError struct {
143	    Code    int
144	    Message string
145	}
146	
147	const (
148	    ExitOK          = 0
149	    ExitError       = 1
150	    ExitUsage       = 2   // Bad command/flag usage
151	    ExitPermission  = 3   // sudo/permission denied
152	    ExitNetwork     = 4   // Network unreachable
153	    ExitNotFound    = 5   // Resource not found
154	)
155	```
156	
157	In `--json` mode, errors are emitted as `{"error": {"code": 3, "message": "..."}}`.
158	
159	### Command Flow
160	
161	```
162	user types: mac wifi on
163	  -> Cobra root command parses args
164	  -> Registry looks up "wifi" module
165	  -> Module.Commands() returns all wifi commands
166	  -> Cobra matches "on" subcommand
167	  -> Command.Run(ctx) executes
168	  -> wifi.Run() calls platform.SetWiFi(true)
169	  -> ctx.Output prints result (colored, --json, or quiet)
170	```
171	
172	### Adding a New Module
173	
174	1. Create `internal/modules/<name>/<name>.go`
175	2. Implement `Module` interface (4 methods)
176	3. Add blank import in `cmd/mac/main.go`: `_ "supermac/internal/modules/<name>"`
177	4. Auto-registered via `init()` — help text, completions, all generated
178	
179	No dispatcher file edits. No category map drift (BUG-004 class bug impossible).
180	
181	## Platform Abstraction
182	
183	The test seam that makes 80% coverage possible. Modules never call `exec.Command`
184	directly — they call `ctx.Platform` methods.
185	
186	```go
187	// internal/platform/platform.go
188	type Interface interface {
189	    // osascript
190	    RunOSAScript(script string) (string, error)
191	
192	    // defaults read/write/delete
193	    ReadDefault(domain, key string) (string, error)
194	    WriteDefault(domain, key, value string) error
195	    DeleteDefault(domain, key string) error
196	
197	    // Network
198	    SetWiFi(on bool) error
199	    GetWiFiStatus() (*WiFiInfo, error)
200	    ScanWiFiNetworks() ([]Network, error)
201	    FlushDNS() error                // sudo-gated
202	    ResetNetwork() error            // sudo-gated
203	
204	    // System
205	    GetMemoryInfo() (*MemoryInfo, error)
206	    GetCPUInfo() (*CPUInfo, error)
207	    GetBatteryInfo() (*BatteryInfo, error)
208	    GetHardwareInfo() (*HardwareInfo, error)
209	    GetPageSize() (int, error)
210	
211	    // Display
212	    SetBrightness(level float64) error
213	    GetDarkMode() (bool, error)
214	    SetDarkMode(on bool) error
215	
216	    // Audio
217	    GetVolume() (int, error)
218	    SetVolume(level int) error
219	    GetAudioDevices() ([]AudioDevice, error)
220	
221	    // Process management
222	    ListProcesses(filter string) ([]Process, error)
223	    KillPort(port int) error
224	    GetPortUser(port int) (string, error)
225	
226	    // General command execution
227	    RunCommand(name string, args ...string) (string, error)
228	    RunSudoCommand(name string, args ...string) (string, error)
229	}
230	```
231	
232	### sudo-gated Operations
233	
234	sudo operations use `ctx.Platform.RunSudoCommand()` which:
235	1. Checks if the current user has passwordless sudo (`sudo -n true`)
236	2. If yes: runs the command directly
237	3. If no: shells out to `sudo` which prompts the user natively
238	4. In `--dry-run` mode: prints the command that would run, returns success
239	5. In tests: `MockPlatform.RunSudoCommand()` is a no-op that records the call
240	
241	Modules do not know about sudo — they just call platform methods. The platform
242	layer handles privilege escalation transparently.
243	
244	### External Tool Dependencies
245	
246	| Bash dependency | Go replacement | Status |
247	|----------------|----------------|--------|
248	| `python3 -m http.server` | `net/http` | Eliminated by Go |
249	| `uuidgen` | `github.com/google/uuid` | Eliminated by Go |
250	| `base64` | `encoding/base64` | Eliminated by Go |
251	| `jq` | `encoding/json` | Eliminated by Go |
252	| `osascript` | `ctx.Platform.RunOSAScript()` | Kept — no Go equivalent |
253	| `defaults` | `ctx.Platform.ReadDefault()` | Kept — no Go equivalent |
254	| `networksetup` | `ctx.Platform.SetWiFi()` | Kept — no Go equivalent |
255	| `airport` | `ctx.Platform.GetWiFiStatus()` | Kept — no Go equivalent |
256	| `system_profiler` | `ctx.Platform.GetHardwareInfo()` | Kept — no Go equivalent |
257	| `SwitchAudioSource` | Optional — detect at runtime | Keep as optional dep |
258	| `dockutil` | Optional — detect at runtime | Keep as optional dep |
259	
260	External tools that can't be replaced return a friendly error:
261	`"This command requires SwitchAudioSource. Install with: brew install switchaudio-osx"`
262	
263	## Config System
264	
265	```yaml
266	# ~/.supermac/config.yaml
267	version: 1
268	
269	output:
270	  color: true
271	  format: text         # text | json | quiet
272	
273	updates:
274	  check: true
275	  channel: stable      # stable | beta
276	
277	modules:
278	  screenshot:
279	    location: Desktop
280	    format: PNG
281	    shadow: false
282	  audio:
283	    volume_step: 10
284	  display:
285	    brightness_step: 10
286	
287	aliases:
288	  kp: "dev kill-port"
289	  dark: "display dark-mode"
290	```
291	
292	- First run creates config with sensible defaults
293	- Modules read their section: `ctx.Config.Modules.Screenshot.Location`
294	- CLI flags override config: `--json` beats `output.format: text`
295	- `mac config edit` opens in `$EDITOR`
296	- `mac config get <key>` / `mac config set <key> <value>` for scripting
297	- Aliases are single source of truth (no drift with hardcoded map)
298	- YAML for comments and readability (standard in Go CLIs)
299	
300	### Config Migration (Bash → Go)
301	
302	On first run, if `~/.supermac/config.yaml` doesn't exist but
303	`~/.supermac/config.json` does (from Bash version):
304	1. Read old JSON config
305	2. Convert to new YAML schema (alias format: `category:action` → `category action`)
306	3. Write `~/.supermac/config.yaml`
307	4. Rename old `config.json` to `config.json.bak`
308	5. Print migration notice
309	
310	### Global Shortcuts & Built-in Commands
311	
312	Config aliases are converted to Cobra aliases at registration time:
313	
314	```go
315	// During Cobra command setup, for each alias in config:
316	rootCmd.AddCommand(&cobra.Command{
317	    Use: aliasName,
318	    Run: func(cmd *cobra.Command, args []string) {
319	        // Parse "dev kill-port" → route to dev module, kill-port action
320	    },
321	})
322	```
323	
324	Built-in commands (not part of any module):
325	- `mac help` / `mac help <category>` — Help system
326	- `mac version` / `mac -v` — Version info
327	- `mac search <term>` — Full-text search across modules
328	- `mac config edit/get/set/list` — Config management
329	- `mac update` / `mac update --check` — Self-update
330	- `mac completion <shell>` — Shell completions
331	
332	## Output System
333	
334	```go
335	type Writer interface {
336	    Info(msg string)
337	    Success(msg string)
338	    Warning(msg string)
339	    Error(msg string)
340	    Header(title string)
341	    Table(rows []Row)
342	    JSON(v interface{})
343	}
344	```
345	
346	- `--json`: structured JSON output for all commands (scripting, desktop API)
347	- `--quiet`: suppress all output except errors
348	- `--no-color`: strip ANSI (also respects `NO_COLOR` env var)
349	- Non-TTY auto-detection: disables colors and spinners when piped
350	
351	## Auto-Update
352	
353	- Background goroutine checks GitHub Releases API on launch
354	- Compares semver, notifies user if newer
355	- `mac update --check` shows available version only
356	- Respects config: `updates.check: false` disables
357	
358	### Atomic Self-Replace
359	
360	`mac update` uses a two-step atomic swap on macOS:
361	
362	1. Download new binary to temp file
363	2. Verify SHA256 against checksums fetched from GitHub Release
364	3. Checksums verified against signed provenance attestation (GitHub attestations)
365	4. `mv current_binary current_binary.bak` (preserve old version)
366	5. `mv temp_file current_binary` (atomic rename on same filesystem)
367	6. `chmod +x current_binary`
368	7. If step 4-6 fail: rollback by restoring `.bak`
369	8. On next run: `.bak` file cleaned up if new version starts successfully
370	
371	### Code Signing & Notarization
372	
373	Release binaries must be:
374	- Signed: `codesign -s "Developer ID Application: CosmoLabs" mac`
375	- Notarized: `notarytool submit mac.zip --wait`
376	- Stapled: `xnotary staple mac`
377	
378	macOS Gatekeeper will reject unsigned binaries. Notarization is required for
379	users who don't Homebrew install (Homebrew handles Gatekeeper differently).
380	
381	### Checksum Integrity
382	
383	SHA256SUMS file is:
384	- Generated at release time by CI
385	- Signed with GPG or GitHub attestation
386	- Fetched from the same GitHub Release but verified against the attestation
387	- Not just "matching hash" — provenance must be verifiable
388	
389	## Versioning
390	
391	Single source of truth in `internal/version/version.go`:
392	
393	```go
394	var Version = "dev"
395	```
396	
397	Stamped at build time via ldflags:
398	
399	```
400	go build -ldflags "-X internal/version.Version=0.2.0" ./cmd/mac
401	```
402	
403	Used by `mac version`, auto-update checker, and `--version` flag.
404	
405	## Testing
406	
407	- Each module testable in isolation via `ctx.Platform` (mock or real)
408	- `platform.Interface` is the test seam with ~25 methods covering all macOS calls
409	- Unit tests: mock platform, test module logic in isolation
410	- Integration tests: real platform calls on macOS CI runner
411	- Target: 80%+ unit coverage for modules, integration coverage for platform/
412	- `go test ./...` for everything, `go test ./internal/modules/wifi/...` for one
413	- CI: GitHub Actions on macOS runner (needs real osascript, defaults, etc.)
414	- `platform/` package itself has lower coverage (makes real system calls) — this
415	  is acceptable. The mock boundary is at the module/platform interface.
416	
417	## Distribution
418	
419	### Multi-channel
420	
421	1. **GitHub Releases**: Binary + checksums per platform (darwin-amd64, darwin-arm64)
422	2. **Install script**: `curl -fsSL cosmolabs.org/install | bash` — detects arch, verifies checksum
423	3. **Homebrew tap**: `brew install cosmolabs-org/tap/supermac`
424	4. **npm/bun**: `bun install -g supermac` — thin wrapper downloading Go binary
425	
426	### Makefile
427	
428	```makefile
429	make build          # Current OS/arch
430	make build-all      # Cross-compile darwin-amd64, darwin-arm64
431	make install        # go install to GOPATH/bin
432	make test           # go test ./...
433	make lint           # golangci-lint
434	make completions    # Generate bash/zsh/fish
435	make release        # Tag + GitHub Release with checksums
436	```
437	
438	### Release Artifacts
439	
440	- `supermac_vX.Y.Z_darwin_arm64.tar.gz` (binary + completions)
441	- `supermac_vX.Y.Z_darwin_amd64.tar.gz`
442	- `SHA256SUMS` (signed)
443	
444	## Key Dependencies
445	
446	| Package | Purpose |
447	|---------|---------|
448	| `github.com/spf13/cobra` | CLI framework (subcommands, help, completions) |
449	| `gopkg.in/yaml.v3` | Config file parsing |
450	| `golang.org/x/crypto` | SHA256 verification for downloads |
451	| `github.com/blang/semver` | Semantic version comparison |
452	
453	## Constraints
454	
455	- **macOS only** for now (uses osascript, defaults, airport, system_profiler)
456	- **No cgo** — pure Go for easy cross-compilation
457	- **Single binary** — no external dependencies at runtime
458	- **Bash compatibility** — same command interface, users can swap seamlessly
459	- **`pkg/` starts empty** — public API populated only after internal API stabilizes
460	
461	## Future (Out of Scope for Initial Rewrite)
462	
463	- Desktop application (SwiftUI wrapping the Go CLI)
464	- Bubble Tea TUI for rich interactive commands
465	- Linux support (would need platform abstraction layer)
466	- Plugin system for third-party modules
467	- Homebrew-core submission (requires community adoption)
468

file.content:
# SuperMac Go Rewrite Design

**Status**: Approved (revised after spec review)
**Date**: 2026-04-08
**Author**: GAB (CosmoLabs)
**Scope**: Full CLI rewrite from Bash to Go
**Review**: 4 critical, 7 medium, 6 low issues found — critical addressed below

## Overview

Rewrite SuperMac from Bash to Go. 1:1 port of all 10 modules maintaining the
same CLI interface (`mac <category> <action>`). Go-native improvements:
`--json` output, auto-generated shell completions, self-update, real config
system. The Go CLI becomes the engine for a future desktop application.

## Architecture

### Approach: Cobra + Package-per-Module

Cobra for CLI backbone (subcommands, help, completions, man pages). Each
module is an isolated Go package implementing a shared interface. Platform
calls (osascript, defaults, airport) centralized in a `platform` package.

### Project Structure

```
supermac/
  cmd/
    mac/main.go              Entrypoint (wires Cobra root command)
  internal/
    module/
      module.go              Module interface: Name, Commands, Help
      registry.go            Auto-discovers and registers modules
    modules/
      wifi/wifi.go           Implements module.Interface
      system/system.go
      network/network.go
      display/display.go
      dock/dock.go
      finder/finder.go
      dev/dev.go
      audio/audio.go
      screenshot/screenshot.go
    config/
      config.go              Loads ~/.supermac/config.yaml
    output/
      output.go              Colored, JSON, quiet formatters
      colors.go              ANSI color helpers
    update/
      update.go              Check GitHub Releases, prompt/apply
    platform/
      airport.go             macOS WiFi binary wrapper
      defaults.go            defaults read/write wrappers
      osascript.go           AppleScript bridge
    version/
      version.go             Single source of truth (stamped via ldflags)
  pkg/
    supermac/                Public API (for desktop app later)
  completions/               Generated shell completion scripts
  config.example.yaml        Sample config with comments
  Makefile                   build, install, test, lint, release
  go.mod
  go.sum
```

Key boundary: `internal/` is private to the CLI (can change freely). `pkg/`
is the stable public API the desktop app will consume.

## Module Interface

```go
// internal/module/module.go

type Module interface {
    Name() string
    ShortDescription() string
    Emoji() string
    Commands() []Command
    Search(term string) []SearchResult  // Full-text search across module commands
}

type Command struct {
    Name        string
    Description string
    Aliases     []string
    Args        []Arg
    Flags       []Flag                  // Per-command flags (e.g., --sort, --force)
    Run         func(ctx *Context) error
}

type Flag struct {
    Name         string
    Shorthand    string                 // Single char, e.g., "s" for --sort
    Description  string
    DefaultValue string
    Required     bool
}

type Arg struct {
    Name        string
    Required    bool
    Description string
}

type Context struct {
    Config   *config.Config
    Output   output.Writer
    Platform platform.Interface       // Injected — real or mock
    Prompt   PromptInterface          // Interactive confirmation (mockable)
    Args     []string
    Flags    map[string]string        // Parsed flag values
    Verbose  bool
    DryRun   bool
}

type SearchResult struct {
    Command     string
    Description string
    Module      string
}
```

### Interactive Prompts

```go
// internal/module/prompt.go
type PromptInterface interface {
    Confirm(msg string) (bool, error)       // Y/n dialog
    Input(msg string) (string, error)       // Free text input
    Select(msg string, opts []string) (int, error) // Choice selection
}
```

Destructive operations (`system cleanup`, `network reset`, `dock reset`) use
`ctx.Prompt.Confirm()` which can be mocked in tests. `--yes` flag sets a
non-interactive Prompt implementation that always returns true.

### Error Handling

```go
// internal/module/errors.go
type ExitError struct {
    Code    int
    Message string
}

const (
    ExitOK          = 0
    ExitError       = 1
    ExitUsage       = 2   // Bad command/flag usage
    ExitPermission  = 3   // sudo/permission denied
    ExitNetwork     = 4   // Network unreachable
    ExitNotFound    = 5   // Resource not found
)
```

In `--json` mode, errors are emitted as `{"error": {"code": 3, "message": "..."}}`.

### Command Flow

```
user types: mac wifi on
  -> Cobra root command parses args
  -> Registry looks up "wifi" module
  -> Module.Commands() returns all wifi commands
  -> Cobra matches "on" subcommand
  -> Command.Run(ctx) executes
  -> wifi.Run() calls platform.SetWiFi(true)
  -> ctx.Output prints result (colored, --json, or quiet)
```

### Adding a New Module

1. Create `internal/modules/<name>/<name>.go`
2. Implement `Module` interface (4 methods)
3. Add blank import in `cmd/mac/main.go`: `_ "supermac/internal/modules/<name>"`
4. Auto-registered via `init()` — help text, completions, all generated

No dispatcher file edits. No category map drift (BUG-004 class bug impossible).

## Platform Abstraction

The test seam that makes 80% coverage possible. Modules never call `exec.Command`
directly — they call `ctx.Platform` methods.

```go
// internal/platform/platform.go
type Interface interface {
    // osascript
    RunOSAScript(script string) (string, error)

    // defaults read/write/delete
    ReadDefault(domain, key string) (string, error)
    WriteDefault(domain, key, value string) error
    DeleteDefault(domain, key string) error

    // Network
    SetWiFi(on bool) error
    GetWiFiStatus() (*WiFiInfo, error)
    ScanWiFiNetworks() ([]Network, error)
    FlushDNS() error                // sudo-gated
    ResetNetwork() error            // sudo-gated

    // System
    GetMemoryInfo() (*MemoryInfo, error)
    GetCPUInfo() (*CPUInfo, error)
    GetBatteryInfo() (*BatteryInfo, error)
    GetHardwareInfo() (*HardwareInfo, error)
    GetPageSize() (int, error)

    // Display
    SetBrightness(level float64) error
    GetDarkMode() (bool, error)
    SetDarkMode(on bool) error

    // Audio
    GetVolume() (int, error)
    SetVolume(level int) error
    GetAudioDevices() ([]AudioDevice, error)

    // Process management
    ListProcesses(filter string) ([]Process, error)
    KillPort(port int) error
    GetPortUser(port int) (string, error)

    // General command execution
    RunCommand(name string, args ...string) (string, error)
    RunSudoCommand(name string, args ...string) (string, error)
}
```

### sudo-gated Operations

sudo operations use `ctx.Platform.RunSudoCommand()` which:
1. Checks if the current user has passwordless sudo (`sudo -n true`)
2. If yes: runs the command directly
3. If no: shells out to `sudo` which prompts the user natively
4. In `--dry-run` mode: prints the command that would run, returns success
5. In tests: `MockPlatform.RunSudoCommand()` is a no-op that records the call

Modules do not know about sudo — they just call platform methods. The platform
layer handles privilege escalation transparently.

### External Tool Dependencies

| Bash dependency | Go replacement | Status |
|----------------|----------------|--------|
| `python3 -m http.server` | `net/http` | Eliminated by Go |
| `uuidgen` | `github.com/google/uuid` | Eliminated by Go |
| `base64` | `encoding/base64` | Eliminated by Go |
| `jq` | `encoding/json` | Eliminated by Go |
| `osascript` | `ctx.Platform.RunOSAScript()` | Kept — no Go equivalent |
| `defaults` | `ctx.Platform.ReadDefault()` | Kept — no Go equivalent |
| `networksetup` | `ctx.Platform.SetWiFi()` | Kept — no Go equivalent |
| `airport` | `ctx.Platform.GetWiFiStatus()` | Kept — no Go equivalent |
| `system_profiler` | `ctx.Platform.GetHardwareInfo()` | Kept — no Go equivalent |
| `SwitchAudioSource` | Optional — detect at runtime | Keep as optional dep |
| `dockutil` | Optional — detect at runtime | Keep as optional dep |

External tools that can't be replaced return a friendly error:
`"This command requires SwitchAudioSource. Install with: brew install switchaudio-osx"`

## Config System

```yaml
# ~/.supermac/config.yaml
version: 1

output:
  color: true
  format: text         # text | json | quiet

updates:
  check: true
  channel: stable      # stable | beta

modules:
  screenshot:
    location: Desktop
    format: PNG
    shadow: false
  audio:
    volume_step: 10
  display:
    brightness_step: 10

aliases:
  kp: "dev kill-port"
  dark: "display dark-mode"
```

- First run creates config with sensible defaults
- Modules read their section: `ctx.Config.Modules.Screenshot.Location`
- CLI flags override config: `--json` beats `output.format: text`
- `mac config edit` opens in `$EDITOR`
- `mac config get <key>` / `mac config set <key> <value>` for scripting
- Aliases are single source of truth (no drift with hardcoded map)
- YAML for comments and readability (standard in Go CLIs)

### Config Migration (Bash → Go)

On first run, if `~/.supermac/config.yaml` doesn't exist but
`~/.supermac/config.json` does (from Bash version):
1. Read old JSON config
2. Convert to new YAML schema (alias format: `category:action` → `category action`)
3. Write `~/.supermac/config.yaml`
4. Rename old `config.json` to `config.json.bak`
5. Print migration notice

### Global Shortcuts & Built-in Commands

Config aliases are converted to Cobra aliases at registration time:

```go
// During Cobra command setup, for each alias in config:
rootCmd.AddCommand(&cobra.Command{
    Use: aliasName,
    Run: func(cmd *cobra.Command, args []string) {
        // Parse "dev kill-port" → route to dev module, kill-port action
    },
})
```

Built-in commands (not part of any module):
- `mac help` / `mac help <category>` — Help system
- `mac version` / `mac -v` — Version info
- `mac search <term>` — Full-text search across modules
- `mac config edit/get/set/list` — Config management
- `mac update` / `mac update --check` — Self-update
- `mac completion <shell>` — Shell completions

## Output System

```go
type Writer interface {
    Info(msg string)
    Success(msg string)
    Warning(msg string)
    Error(msg string)
    Header(title string)
    Table(rows []Row)
    JSON(v interface{})
}
```

- `--json`: structured JSON output for all commands (scripting, desktop API)
- `--quiet`: suppress all output except errors
- `--no-color`: strip ANSI (also respects `NO_COLOR` env var)
- Non-TTY auto-detection: disables colors and spinners when piped

## Auto-Update

- Background goroutine checks GitHub Releases API on launch
- Compares semver, notifies user if newer
- `mac update --check` shows available version only
- Respects config: `updates.check: false` disables

### Atomic Self-Replace

`mac update` uses a two-step atomic swap on macOS:

1. Download new binary to temp file
2. Verify SHA256 against checksums fetched from GitHub Release
3. Checksums verified against signed provenance attestation (GitHub attestations)
4. `mv current_binary current_binary.bak` (preserve old version)
5. `mv temp_file current_binary` (atomic rename on same filesystem)
6. `chmod +x current_binary`
7. If step 4-6 fail: rollback by restoring `.bak`
8. On next run: `.bak` file cleaned up if new version starts successfully

### Code Signing & Notarization

Release binaries must be:
- Signed: `codesign -s "Developer ID Application: CosmoLabs" mac`
- Notarized: `notarytool submit mac.zip --wait`
- Stapled: `xnotary staple mac`

macOS Gatekeeper will reject unsigned binaries. Notarization is required for
users who don't Homebrew install (Homebrew handles Gatekeeper differently).

### Checksum Integrity

SHA256SUMS file is:
- Generated at release time by CI
- Signed with GPG or GitHub attestation
- Fetched from the same GitHub Release but verified against the attestation
- Not just "matching hash" — provenance must be verifiable

## Versioning

Single source of truth in `internal/version/version.go`:

```go
var Version = "dev"
```

Stamped at build time via ldflags:

```
go build -ldflags "-X internal/version.Version=0.2.0" ./cmd/mac
```

Used by `mac version`, auto-update checker, and `--version` flag.

## Testing

- Each module testable in isolation via `ctx.Platform` (mock or real)
- `platform.Interface` is the test seam with ~25 methods covering all macOS calls
- Unit tests: mock platform, test module logic in isolation
- Integration tests: real platform calls on macOS CI runner
- Target: 80%+ unit coverage for modules, integration coverage for platform/
- `go test ./...` for everything, `go test ./internal/modules/wifi/...` for one
- CI: GitHub Actions on macOS runner (needs real osascript, defaults, etc.)
- `platform/` package itself has lower coverage (makes real system calls) — this
  is acceptable. The mock boundary is at the module/platform interface.

## Distribution

### Multi-channel

1. **GitHub Releases**: Binary + checksums per platform (darwin-amd64, darwin-arm64)
2. **Install script**: `curl -fsSL cosmolabs.org/install | bash` — detects arch, verifies checksum
3. **Homebrew tap**: `brew install cosmolabs-org/tap/supermac`
4. **npm/bun**: `bun install -g supermac` — thin wrapper downloading Go binary

### Makefile

```makefile
make build          # Current OS/arch
make build-all      # Cross-compile darwin-amd64, darwin-arm64
make install        # go install to GOPATH/bin
make test           # go test ./...
make lint           # golangci-lint
make completions    # Generate bash/zsh/fish
make release        # Tag + GitHub Release with checksums
```

### Release Artifacts

- `supermac_vX.Y.Z_darwin_arm64.tar.gz` (binary + completions)
- `supermac_vX.Y.Z_darwin_amd64.tar.gz`
- `SHA256SUMS` (signed)

## Key Dependencies

| Package | Purpose |
|---------|---------|
| `github.com/spf13/cobra` | CLI framework (subcommands, help, completions) |
| `gopkg.in/yaml.v3` | Config file parsing |
| `golang.org/x/crypto` | SHA256 verification for downloads |
| `github.com/blang/semver` | Semantic version comparison |

## Constraints

- **macOS only** for now (uses osascript, defaults, airport, system_profiler)
- **No cgo** — pure Go for easy cross-compilation
- **Single binary** — no external dependencies at runtime
- **Bash compatibility** — same command interface, users can swap seamlessly
- **`pkg/` starts empty** — public API populated only after internal API stabilizes

## Future (Out of Scope for Initial Rewrite)

- Desktop application (SwiftUI wrapping the Go CLI)
- Bubble Tea TUI for rich interactive commands
- Linux support (would need platform abstraction layer)
- Plugin system for third-party modules
- Homebrew-core submission (requires community adoption)

input.content:
package platform

// Interface is the test seam for all macOS system calls.
// Modules never call exec.Command directly — they use this interface.
type Interface interface {
	// osascript
	RunOSAScript(script string) (string, error)

	// defaults read/write/delete
	ReadDefault(domain, key string) (string, error)
	WriteDefault(domain, key, value string) error
	DeleteDefault(domain, key string) error

	// Network
	SetWiFi(on bool) error
	GetWiFiStatus() (*WiFiInfo, error)
	ScanWiFiNetworks() ([]Network, error)
	FlushDNS() error
	ResetNetwork() error

	// System
	GetMemoryInfo() (*MemoryInfo, error)
	GetCPUInfo() (*CPUInfo, error)
	GetBatteryInfo() (*BatteryInfo, error)
	GetHardwareInfo() (*HardwareInfo, error)
	GetPageSize() (int, error)

	// Display
	SetBrightness(level float64) error
	GetDarkMode() (bool, error)
	SetDarkMode(on bool) error

	// Audio
	GetVolume() (int, error)
	SetVolume(level int) error
	GetAudioDevices() ([]AudioDevice, error)

	// Process management
	ListProcesses(filter string) ([]Process, error)
	KillPort(port int) error
	GetPortUser(port int) (string, error)

	// General command execution
	RunCommand(name string, args ...string) (string, error)
	RunSudoCommand(name string, args ...string) (string, error)
}

// Data types returned by platform methods.

type WiFiInfo struct {
	SSID     string
	BSSID    string
	Signal   int
	Channel  string
	Connected bool
}

type Network struct {
	SSID   string
	Signal int
	Security string
}

type MemoryInfo struct {
	Total     uint64
	Used      uint64
	Free      uint64
	Active    uint64
	Inactive  uint64
	Wired     uint64
	Compressed uint64
	SwapTotal uint64
	SwapUsed  uint64
}

type CPUInfo struct {
	Model     string
	Cores     int
	Threads   int
	Usage     float64
}

type BatteryInfo struct {
	Percent      int
	Charging     bool
	Health       string
	CycleCount   int
	TimeRemaining string
}

type HardwareInfo struct {
	Model       string
	Chip        string
	Memory      string
	Serial      string
	OSVersion   string
	Build       string
	Arch        string
}

type AudioDevice struct {
	ID    string
	Name  string
	Type  string // input, output
	Active bool
}

type Process struct {
	PID     int
	User    string
	CPU     float64
	Memory  float64
	Command string
}

content.content: Launching skill: commit-all

content.text:
# Commit-All v2 — CCS-Delegated Intelligent Commit Workflow

CCS handles mechanical scanning and grouping. Claude handles creative message writing and review decisions.

## Usage

```bash
/commit-all                    # Analyze and commit all changes
/commit-all --session          # Only changes from this session
/commit-all master --push      # Commit, merge to master, push
/commit-all --skip-review      # Skip code review for trivial changes
/commit-all --skip-quality     # Skip build/lint/test checks
```

## Execution

### Phase 0: WORKCHECK (optional, 2 seconds)

If an active continuation prompt exists, run `ccs workcheck` to surface any unverified goals or documentation gaps before committing. Display the output to the user but do NOT block on it — proceed to Phase 1 regardless.

Skip if: `--skip-quality` flag is set, or no active prompt exists (`ccs workcheck` returns error).

### Phase 1: ANALYZE (CCS — fast, zero tokens)

Run `ccs commit-analyze --json` (or `--json --session` if `--session` flag).

This returns structured JSON with:
- File changes grouped by semantic proximity
- Quality gate results (build/lint/test)
- Diff summaries per group (regex-extracted function/type/test names)
- Issue references (auto-detected from `docs/issues/` affected_files)
- `review_recommended: true` if total diff > 100 lines

If `--skip-quality` was passed, add `--skip-quality` flag.

### Phase 2: REVIEW + REGROUP (Claude — creative)

Review CCS's suggested groups. For each group, decide:
- Accept the grouping, or reorganize (split/merge) based on session context
- Split groups that mix `feat` and `fix` changes
- Merge groups that are logically the same feature

If `review_recommended` is true and `--skip-review` not set: invoke `superpowers:requesting-code-review` for architectural/security review.

If 3+ groups, create a TaskCreate per group for progress visibility.

### Phase 3: CRAFT MESSAGES (Claude — the main value)

**Principle: Commit messages are permanent history. You're not losing history — you're building it.**

Write commit messages for each group following conventional commit standards. Scale detail to significance:

| Type | Subject | Body | Example Body |
|------|---------|------|-------------|
| **feat** | What it enables | Structured bullets: what each component adds, design decisions, test counts | `- Add TokenBucket with per-key limits and automatic cleanup` / `- Design: sliding window over fixed counters for accuracy vs memory` / `- 21 tests covering burst, expiry, and concurrent access` |
| **fix** | What was broken | Root cause + how it's fixed | `Root cause: race condition in session cleanup — goroutine reads map while reaper deletes.` / `Fix: copy keys slice before iteration, delete under write lock.` |
| **test** | What's covered | Edge cases, why these tests matter | `- 14 tests covering: valid input, boundary values, nil handling, concurrent writes` / `- Regression test for BUG-045 (duplicate session IDs)` |
| **refactor** | What improved | Before/after reasoning | `Before: 340-line monolith handling parse + validate + emit.` / `After: three focused functions, each independently testable.` |
| **perf** | What's optimized + metric | Before/after numbers | `Reduce CLAUDE.md from 461 to 350 lines (~24% reduction, ~660 tokens saved)` |
| **security** | What's hardened | Vulnerability + fix | `Root cause: unsanitized input interpolated into sh -c. Fix: validate via allowlist before interpolation.` |
| **docs** | What's documented | Concise, 1-line body max | `Update API reference for v2 auth endpoints.` |
| **ci** | What pipeline changed | Brief explanation | `Add ast-grep scan step to pre-commit checks.` |
| **build** | What build changed | Brief explanation | `Update Makefile port assignments for monorepo services.` |
| **chore** | What changed | No body needed for metadata | *(none)* |

#### Format Rules

**Subject**: `type(scope): imperative summary` — max 72 chars, lowercase after colon.
**Body**: Blank line after subject. Imperative bullet points: "Add X", "Fix Y", "Remove Z" — never paragraphs.
**Footer**: `Refs: FEAT-xxx` when CCS detected issue refs. `BREAKING CHANGE:` when applicable.

#### Mandatory Rules

- **Never** produce a single-line message for `feat` commits touching 3+ files — the body is required.
- **Always** include the WHY for features: what does this enable? What problem does it solve?
- **Always** include test counts when tests are added: "21 tests covering..." not just "add tests".
- **Always** reference issues/roadmap items from CCS analysis in footer: `Refs: FEAT-xxx, ROAD-yyy`.
- **No AI attribution** — no co-authored-by, no emoji signatures, no "Generated with" lines.
- **No prose paragraphs** — bullets only. Each bullet starts with an imperative verb.

#### Examples

**Bad — too thin** (no body for a multi-file feature):
```
feat(crypto): add crypto module
```

**Bad — over-engineered** (prose wall, repeats the diff):
```
feat(crypto): add crypto module

This commit introduces a comprehensive cryptographic module that provides
encryption and decryption capabilities. The module was designed with careful
consideration of security best practices and performance requirements. We
evaluated several approaches including AES-GCM, ChaCha20-Poly1305, and
XSalsa20 before settling on AES-256-GCM as the primary cipher suite due
to hardware acceleration support on modern processors...
(200 more words)
```

**Good — structured, scaled to significance**:
```
feat(crypto): add AES-256-GCM encryption module

- Add Encrypt/Decrypt with authenticated data support
- Add key derivation from passphrase via Argon2id
- Design: AES-GCM over ChaCha20 for hardware acceleration on target infra
- 18 tests covering: roundtrip, bad key, tampered ciphertext, empty input

Refs: FEAT-091
```

**Good — fix with root cause**:
```
fix(reaper): prevent panic on concurrent session cleanup

Root cause: goroutine iterates session map while reaper deletes entries.
Fix: snapshot keys under read lock, delete individually under write lock.

Refs: BUG-072
```

**Good — chore, no body needed**:
```
chore: update Go dependencies to latest patch versions
```

### Integration Check (Advisory)

If an `integration-manifest.yaml` exists in the project root or `docs/`, and the staged changes span 2+ platform directories (check with `git diff --cached --name-only` against the manifest's platform paths):

```
⚠️ Changes span {platform1} + {platform2} — consider: ccs audit integration --quick
```

This is advisory only — display the suggestion, do not auto-run. Skip if no manifest exists.

### Phase 4: EXECUTE (CCS — fast)

Build JSON plan and pipe to `ccs commit-batch`:
```json
[
  {"files": ["path/file1.go", "path/file2.go"], "message": "feat(scope): subject\n\nBody"},
  {"files": ["path/file3_test.go"], "message": "test(scope): subject"}
]
```

Run: `echo '<plan>' | ccs commit-batch --json`

Or with `--dry-run` first to preview.

### Phase 5: POST (optional)

- If target branch specified: `ccs merge <target>`
- If `--push`: `ccs sync --push`

## Output

```
✅ Created N semantic commits
   1. abc1234 feat(auth): implement JWT authentication
   2. def5678 docs(auth): document auth endpoints
   3. ghi9012 test(auth): add auth coverage

[If merged:] ✅ Merged to: master
[If pushed:] ✅ Pushed to remote
```

## Note on Versioning

Version bumps are NOT automatic. Use `/release` for proper releases.

## Related

`/release` • `/pit-stop` • `/merge` • `ccs commit-analyze` • `ccs commit-batch`

content.text:
# /workcheck — Mid-Session Goal Verification

Answers: **"Did I finish what I started?"** Goes back to the original plan and verifies each goal against TaskList + git evidence.

## Usage

```bash
/workcheck              # Full verification against active prompt
```

## Execution

### Step 1: Gather Data

Run in parallel:
1. `ccs workcheck --json` via Bash
2. TaskList

### Step 2: Read Plan Sources

From the JSON, read `prompt_path`, `brainstorm_ref`, `plan_ref`. Skip missing refs.

### Step 3: Verify Each Goal

Match each goal against TaskList subjects, commit messages, and files changed.
Classify: DONE | PARTIAL | NOT STARTED | BLOCKED

### Step 4: Detect Drift

Commits matching no goal = NEW (unplanned work).

### Step 5: Auto-File Discoveries

File genuine bugs/ideas found during verification.

### Step 6: Output

Structured table with per-goal status, drift section, doc gaps, and recommendation.

## Related

`/remind-me` | `/sweep` | `/what-now` · `/triage` (recommended: lifecycle-aware task selection)

finder_dispatch() {
    local action="$1"
    shift  # Remove action from arguments
    
    case "$action" in
        "restart")
            finder_restart
            ;;
        "show-hidden")
            finder_show_hidden
            ;;
        "hide-hidden")
            finder_hide_hidden
            ;;
        "toggle-hidden")
            finder_toggle_hidden
            ;;
        "reveal")
            finder_reveal "$@"
            ;;
        "status")
            finder_status
            ;;
        "help"|"-h"|"--help")
            finder_help
            ;;
        *)
            print_error "Unknown finder action: $action"
            echo ""
            print_info "Available actions: restart, show-hidden, hide-hidden, toggle-hidden, reveal, status"
            print_info "Use 'mac help finder' for detailed information"

<result>The module compiles cleanly. Here is a summary of what was created:

**File**: `/Users/gab/PROJECTS/SuperMac/supermac-go/internal/modules/dock/dock.go`

**Pattern**: Follows the exact same structure as the finder module -- `init()` with `module.Register()`, `Search()` method, and command implementations using `ctx.Platform.ReadDefault()`/`ctx.Platform.WriteDefault()` with domain `com.apple.dock`.

**Commands ported from Bash** (8 total):

| Command | Bash function | Go method | Key details |
|---------|--------------|-----------|-------------|
| `position <left/bottom/right>` | `dock_set_position` | `position()` | Shorthands `l`/`b`/`r` accepted, no-op if already set |
| `autohide <on/off/toggle>` | `dock_set_autohide` | `autohide()` | Toggle reads current state and inverts |
| `size <small/medium/large/N>` | `dock_set_size` | `size()` | Named sizes (32/64/96px) or raw pixel count (16-256) |
| `magnification <on/off/toggle>` | `dock_set_magnification` | `magnification()` | Sets `largesize` to 128 when enabling |
| `magnification-size <N>` | *(part of magnification)* | `magnificationSize()` | Standalone command for pixel value (16-256) |
| `minimize-effect <genie/scale>` | *(in status)* | `minimizeEffect()` | Writes `mineffect` key |
| `status` | `dock_status` | `status()` | Reads all 7 dock settings with defaults |
| `reset` | `dock_reset` | `reset()` | Deletes domain, writes sensible defaults, restarts |

**Design decisions**:
- `restartDock()` uses `exec.Command("killall", "Dock")` directly (not `ctx.Platform.RunCommand`) to match the finder module's pattern for process restarts.
- `parseOnOffToggle()` is a shared helper that any on/off/toggle command reuses.
- `parseSize()` accepts both named sizes and raw integers, matching the user's spec for `<value>` args.
- No `add`/`remove` commands -- those depend on `dockutil` which is an external dependency not suitable for the platform interface.</result>

<result>The module compiles cleanly. Here is a summary of what was created:

**File**: `/Users/gab/PROJECTS/SuperMac/supermac-go/internal/modules/system/system.go`

**Module**: `system` (emoji: screen) -- implements `module.Module` via `init()` + `module.Register()`.

**6 commands ported from Bash**:

| Command | Description | Implementation |
|---------|-------------|----------------|
| `info` | Comprehensive system info | `exec.Command` for `sw_vers`, `uname`, `hostname`, `uptime`, `df` |
| `cleanup` | Deep system cleanup | `ctx.Prompt.Confirm()` gate, then `ctx.Platform.RunCommand`/`RunSudoCommand`/`RunOSAScript` for cache, downloads, trash, logs, Safari, temp files, font caches |
| `battery` | Battery status and health | `ctx.Platform.GetBatteryInfo()` |
| `memory` | Memory usage statistics | `ctx.Platform.GetMemoryInfo()`, with pressure assessment (low/medium/high) |
| `cpu` | CPU usage and information | `ctx.Platform.GetCPUInfo()`, plus load average from `uptime` |
| `hardware` | Hardware specs | `ctx.Platform.GetHardwareInfo()` |

**Key design decisions**:
- `cleanup` uses `ctx.Prompt.Confirm()` before any destructive operation, returning early if cancelled
- `memory` aliases to `mem` (matching the Bash original)
- `Search()` covers command names, descriptions, aliases, plus extra keyword mappings (`power`, `ram`, `processor`, `clean`, `status`) matching the Bash `system_search` function
- All hardware/platform data flows through `ctx.Platform` interface (testable), while `info` uses `exec.Command` directly for system queries that have no platform method yet
- Output formatting uses `ctx.Output.Header`/`Info`/`Success`/`Warning` plus `fmt.Printf` for key-value lines, matching the finder module pattern</result>

<result>The module compiles and passes vet cleanly.

**Created:** `/Users/gab/PROJECTS/SuperMac/supermac-go/internal/modules/wifi/wifi.go`

**What was ported** -- all commands from `lib/wifi.sh` mapping directly to `Module.Commands()` entries:

| Bash function | Go command | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| `wifi_on` | `on` | Uses `ctx.Platform.SetWiFi(true)`, polls up to 10s for power state, then shows current connection |
| `wifi_off` | `off` | Uses `ctx.Platform.SetWiFi(false)`, waits 2s then verifies |
| `wifi_toggle` | `toggle` | Reads power state, delegates to `on` or `off` |
| `wifi_status` | `status` | Shows interface, power, SSID, signal (via `airport -I` parsed), IP address |
| `wifi_scan` | `scan` | Tries `ctx.Platform.ScanWiFiNetworks()` first, falls back to `networksetup -listpreferredwirelessnetworks` |
| `wifi_connect` | `connect` | Accepts optional second arg for password; calls `networksetup -setairportnetwork` |
| `wifi_forget` | `forget` | Calls `networksetup -removepreferredwirelessnetwork` |
| `wifi_info` | `info` | Full status + airport `-I` parsed fields (SSID, BSSID, channel, country) + gateway/DNS via `route`/`scutil` |
| `wifi_list_saved` | `list-saved` | Lists saved networks via `networksetup` |

**Key design decisions:**
- `init()` + `module.Register(&WiFiModule{})` pattern matches the finder example
- Platform interface methods (`SetWiFi`, `ScanWiFiNetworks`, `GetWiFiStatus`) are used where applicable; raw `exec.Command` is used only for commands not covered by the platform interface (connect, forget, list-saved, DNS/gateway lookups)
- `parseAirportInfo()` splits `airport -I` output on the first colon per line to extract key:value pairs, matching the Bash approach
- `Search()` covers command names, descriptions, and synonym matches (wireless, network, remove)
- The `airport` binary path is the constant from the Bash source as specified</result>

| `ports` (alias `list-ports`) | Shows all listening ports + common dev port scan |

- **Includes `Search()`**: Matches on command name and description, consistent with finder/network modules.

input.content:
# SuperMac — Usage Guide

**Professional macOS power tools for the command line.**

`mac <category> <action> [arguments]`

---

## Installation

```bash
# Homebrew (recommended)
brew install cosmolabs-org/tap/supermac

# One-line install
curl -fsSL https://cosmolabs.org/install | bash

# From source
git clone https://github.com/cosmolabs-org/supermac.git
cd supermac/supermac-go && make install
```

## Global Flags

| Flag | Description |
|------|-------------|
| `--json` | Output in JSON format (for scripting) |
| `--quiet` | Suppress all output except errors |
| `--no-color` | Disable color output |
| `--verbose` | Verbose output |
| `--dry-run` | Show what would be done without executing |
| `--yes`, `-y` | Skip confirmation prompts |

## Built-in Commands

```bash
mac version              # Show version and system info
mac help                 # Show help
mac help <category>      # Show commands for a category
mac config list          # Show current configuration
mac config get <key>     # Get a config value
mac config set <key> <val> # Set a config value
mac config edit          # Open config in $EDITOR
```

---

## Modules

### 📁 finder — File Visibility & Finder Management

Control hidden files, Finder restarts, and file revealing.

```bash
mac finder show-hidden       # Show hidden files in Finder
mac finder hide-hidden       # Hide hidden files
mac finder toggle-hidden     # Toggle hidden files visibility
mac finder reveal <path>     # Reveal file in Finder
mac finder restart           # Restart Finder
mac finder status            # Show Finder settings
```

**Examples:**
```bash
mac finder toggle-hidden     # Quick toggle to see/hide dotfiles
mac finder reveal ~/Desktop/screenshot.png  # Open Finder to file
```

---

### 🌐 wifi — WiFi Control & Management

Manage WiFi connections, scan networks, check signal strength.

```bash
mac wifi on                  # Turn WiFi on
mac wifi off                 # Turn WiFi off
mac wifi toggle              # Toggle WiFi state
mac wifi status              # Show current connection info
mac wifi scan                # Scan for available networks
mac wifi connect <network>   # Connect to a network
mac wifi connect <network> <password>  # Connect with password
mac wifi forget <network>    # Forget a saved network
mac wifi info                # Detailed connection information
```

**Examples:**
```bash
mac wifi toggle              # Quick WiFi on/off
mac wifi status --json       # Machine-readable status
mac wifi scan | grep Office  # Find Office network
```

---

### 📡 network — Network Information & Troubleshooting

IP addresses, DNS, connectivity checks, port scanning.

```bash
mac network ip               # Show local and public IP
mac network public-ip        # Show public IP only
mac network dns-flush        # Flush DNS cache (requires sudo)
mac network ping <host>      # Ping a host
mac network ports            # Show listening ports
mac network interfaces       # List network interfaces
mac network status           # Network status overview
mac network reset            # Reset network settings (requires sudo)
```

**Examples:**
```bash
mac network ip --json        # {"local": "192.168.1.5", "public": "203.0.113.1"}
mac network ports            # Show what's listening
mac network ping google.com  # Test connectivity
```

---

### 🖥️ system — System Information & Maintenance

Hardware info, memory stats, battery status, system cleanup.

```bash
mac system info              # System overview (hostname, OS, uptime)
mac system cleanup           # Clean caches, logs, temp files
mac system battery           # Battery status and health
mac system memory            # Memory usage breakdown
mac system cpu               # CPU info and usage
mac system hardware          # Detailed hardware specs
```

**Examples:**
```bash
mac system memory --json     # Machine-readable memory stats
mac system battery           # Check battery health
mac system cleanup --dry-run # See what would be cleaned
mac system cleanup -y        # Skip confirmation prompts
```

---

### 💻 dev — Developer Tools & Utilities

Port management, process monitoring, HTTP serving, UUIDs.

```bash
mac dev kill-port <port>     # Kill process on a port
mac dev ports                # Show all listening ports
mac dev servers              # Show running servers
mac dev localhost <port>     # Start localhost server
mac dev serve <dir> <port>   # Serve a directory over HTTP
mac dev processes            # List running processes
mac dev processes --sort cpu # Sort by CPU usage
mac dev cpu-hogs             # Show top CPU consumers
mac dev memory-hogs          # Show top memory consumers
mac dev uuid                 # Generate a UUID
mac dev env                  # Show environment variables
```

**Examples:**
```bash
mac dev kill-port 3000       # Free up port 3000
mac dev serve . 8080         # Serve current directory
mac dev uuid                 # Generate UUID for scripts
mac dev processes --sort memory --json  # JSON process list
```

---

### 🖥️ display — Display & Appearance Settings

Brightness, dark mode, Night Shift, True Tone, wallpaper.

```bash
mac display brightness <0-100>  # Set brightness level
mac display dark-mode on      # Enable dark mode
mac display dark-mode off     # Disable dark mode
mac display dark-mode toggle  # Toggle dark mode
mac display night-shift on    # Enable Night Shift
mac display night-shift off   # Disable Night Shift
mac display true-tone on      # Enable True Tone
mac display true-tone off     # Disable True Tone
mac display wallpaper <path>  # Set desktop wallpaper
mac display status            # Show display settings
```

**Examples:**
```bash
mac display brightness 50     # Set to 50%
mac display dark-mode toggle  # Quick dark/light toggle
mac display status --json     # All display settings as JSON
```

---

### 🚢 dock — Dock Management & Positioning

Dock position, auto-hide, icon size, magnification, minimize effects.

```bash
mac dock position <left|bottom|right|top>  # Set dock position
mac dock autohide on          # Enable dock auto-hide
mac dock autohide off         # Disable dock auto-hide
mac dock autohide toggle      # Toggle auto-hide
mac dock size <value>         # Set icon size (1-128)
mac dock magnification on     # Enable magnification
mac dock magnification off    # Disable magnification
mac dock magnification toggle # Toggle magnification
mac dock magnification-size <value>  # Set magnified size
mac dock minimize-effect <genie|scale>  # Set minimize effect
mac dock status               # Show dock settings
mac dock reset                # Reset dock to defaults
```

**Examples:**
```bash
mac dock position bottom      # Move dock to bottom
mac dock size 48              # Set icon size
mac dock status               # See all current dock settings
```

---

### 🔊 audio — Audio Control & Device Management

Volume control, mute, device switching.

```bash
mac audio volume              # Show current volume
mac audio volume <0-100>      # Set volume level
mac audio volume up           # Increase volume
mac audio volume down         # Decrease volume
mac audio volume mute         # Mute audio
mac audio volume unmute       # Unmute audio
mac audio volume toggle-mute  # Toggle mute
mac audio devices             # List audio devices
mac audio input-device <name> # Switch input device
mac audio output-device <name> # Switch output device
mac audio status              # Show audio settings
```

**Examples:**
```bash
mac audio volume 50           # Set volume to 50%
mac audio volume up           # Increase by configured step (default: 10)
mac audio devices --json      # List devices as JSON
mac audio output-device "External Speakers"  # Switch output
```

---

### 📸 screenshot — Screenshot Settings & Management

Screenshot location, format, shadow, cursor settings.

```bash
mac screenshot location       # Show save location
mac screenshot location <path> # Set save location
mac screenshot format         # Show current format
mac screenshot format <png|jpg|tiff|gif>  # Set format
mac screenshot shadow on      # Include window shadow
mac screenshot shadow off     # Exclude window shadow
mac screenshot shadow toggle  # Toggle shadow
mac screenshot cursor show    # Show cursor in screenshots
mac screenshot cursor hide    # Hide cursor in screenshots
mac screenshot cursor toggle  # Toggle cursor visibility
mac screenshot naming sequential # Sequential naming (Screenshot 1, 2...)
mac screenshot naming timestamp # Timestamp naming
mac screenshot status         # Show all screenshot settings
mac screenshot reset          # Reset to defaults
```

**Examples:**
```bash
mac screenshot location ~/Pictures/Screenshots  # Custom save path
mac screenshot format jpg     # Use JPEG format
mac screenshot shadow off     # No shadow on window screenshots
mac screenshot status         # See all settings
```

---

## Configuration

SuperMac stores configuration in `~/.supermac/config.yaml`.

```yaml
version: 1

output:
  color: true
  format: text         # text | json | quiet

toolUseResult.content:
# SuperMac — Usage Guide

**Professional macOS power tools for the command line.**

`mac <category> <action> [arguments]`

---

## Installation

```bash
# Homebrew (recommended)
brew install cosmolabs-org/tap/supermac

# One-line install
curl -fsSL https://cosmolabs.org/install | bash

# From source
git clone https://github.com/cosmolabs-org/supermac.git
cd supermac/supermac-go && make install
```

## Global Flags

| Flag | Description |
|------|-------------|
| `--json` | Output in JSON format (for scripting) |
| `--quiet` | Suppress all output except errors |
| `--no-color` | Disable color output |
| `--verbose` | Verbose output |
| `--dry-run` | Show what would be done without executing |
| `--yes`, `-y` | Skip confirmation prompts |

## Built-in Commands

```bash
mac version              # Show version and system info
mac help                 # Show help
mac help <category>      # Show commands for a category
mac config list          # Show current configuration
mac config get <key>     # Get a config value
mac config set <key> <val> # Set a config value
mac config edit          # Open config in $EDITOR
```

---

## Modules

### 📁 finder — File Visibility & Finder Management

Control hidden files, Finder restarts, and file revealing.

```bash
mac finder show-hidden       # Show hidden files in Finder
mac finder hide-hidden       # Hide hidden files
mac finder toggle-hidden     # Toggle hidden files visibility
mac finder reveal <path>     # Reveal file in Finder
mac finder restart           # Restart Finder
mac finder status            # Show Finder settings
```

**Examples:**
```bash
mac finder toggle-hidden     # Quick toggle to see/hide dotfiles
mac finder reveal ~/Desktop/screenshot.png  # Open Finder to file
```

---

### 🌐 wifi — WiFi Control & Management

Manage WiFi connections, scan networks, check signal strength.

```bash
mac wifi on                  # Turn WiFi on
mac wifi off                 # Turn WiFi off
mac wifi toggle              # Toggle WiFi state
mac wifi status              # Show current connection info
mac wifi scan                # Scan for available networks
mac wifi connect <network>   # Connect to a network
mac wifi connect <network> <password>  # Connect with password
mac wifi forget <network>    # Forget a saved network
mac wifi info                # Detailed connection information
```

**Examples:**
```bash
mac wifi toggle              # Quick WiFi on/off
mac wifi status --json       # Machine-readable status
mac wifi scan | grep Office  # Find Office network
```

---

### 📡 network — Network Information & Troubleshooting

IP addresses, DNS, connectivity checks, port scanning.

```bash
mac network ip               # Show local and public IP
mac network public-ip        # Show public IP only
mac network dns-flush        # Flush DNS cache (requires sudo)
mac network ping <host>      # Ping a host
mac network ports            # Show listening ports
mac network interfaces       # List network interfaces
mac network status           # Network status overview
mac network reset            # Reset network settings (requires sudo)
```

**Examples:**
```bash
mac network ip --json        # {"local": "192.168.1.5", "public": "203.0.113.1"}
mac network ports            # Show what's listening
mac network ping google.com  # Test connectivity
```

---

### 🖥️ system — System Information & Maintenance

Hardware info, memory stats, battery status, system cleanup.

```bash
mac system info              # System overview (hostname, OS, uptime)
mac system cleanup           # Clean caches, logs, temp files
mac system battery           # Battery status and health
mac system memory            # Memory usage breakdown
mac system cpu               # CPU info and usage
mac system hardware          # Detailed hardware specs
```

**Examples:**
```bash
mac system memory --json     # Machine-readable memory stats
mac system battery           # Check battery health
mac system cleanup --dry-run # See what would be cleaned
mac system cleanup -y        # Skip confirmation prompts
```

---

### 💻 dev — Developer Tools & Utilities

Port management, process monitoring, HTTP serving, UUIDs.

```bash
mac dev kill-port <port>     # Kill process on a port
mac dev ports                # Show all listening ports
mac dev servers              # Show running servers
mac dev localhost <port>     # Start localhost server
mac dev serve <dir> <port>   # Serve a directory over HTTP
mac dev processes            # List running processes
mac dev processes --sort cpu # Sort by CPU usage
mac dev cpu-hogs             # Show top CPU consumers
mac dev memory-hogs          # Show top memory consumers
mac dev uuid                 # Generate a UUID
mac dev env                  # Show environment variables
```

**Examples:**
```bash
mac dev kill-port 3000       # Free up port 3000
mac dev serve . 8080         # Serve current directory
mac dev uuid                 # Generate UUID for scripts
mac dev processes --sort memory --json  # JSON process list
```

---

### 🖥️ display — Display & Appearance Settings

Brightness, dark mode, Night Shift, True Tone, wallpaper.

```bash
mac display brightness <0-100>  # Set brightness level
mac display dark-mode on      # Enable dark mode
mac display dark-mode off     # Disable dark mode
mac display dark-mode toggle  # Toggle dark mode
mac display night-shift on    # Enable Night Shift
mac display night-shift off   # Disable Night Shift
mac display true-tone on      # Enable True Tone
mac display true-tone off     # Disable True Tone
mac display wallpaper <path>  # Set desktop wallpaper
mac display status            # Show display settings
```

**Examples:**
```bash
mac display brightness 50     # Set to 50%
mac display dark-mode toggle  # Quick dark/light toggle
mac display status --json     # All display settings as JSON
```

---

### 🚢 dock — Dock Management & Positioning

Dock position, auto-hide, icon size, magnification, minimize effects.

```bash
mac dock position <left|bottom|right|top>  # Set dock position
mac dock autohide on          # Enable dock auto-hide
mac dock autohide off         # Disable dock auto-hide
mac dock autohide toggle      # Toggle auto-hide
mac dock size <value>         # Set icon size (1-128)
mac dock magnification on     # Enable magnification
mac dock magnification off    # Disable magnification
mac dock magnification toggle # Toggle magnification
mac dock magnification-size <value>  # Set magnified size
mac dock minimize-effect <genie|scale>  # Set minimize effect
mac dock status               # Show dock settings
mac dock reset                # Reset dock to defaults
```

**Examples:**
```bash
mac dock position bottom      # Move dock to bottom
mac dock size 48              # Set icon size
mac dock status               # See all current dock settings
```

---

### 🔊 audio — Audio Control & Device Management

Volume control, mute, device switching.

```bash
mac audio volume              # Show current volume
mac audio volume <0-100>      # Set volume level
mac audio volume up           # Increase volume
mac audio volume down         # Decrease volume
mac audio volume mute         # Mute audio
mac audio volume unmute       # Unmute audio
mac audio volume toggle-mute  # Toggle mute
mac audio devices             # List audio devices
mac audio input-device <name> # Switch input device
mac audio output-device <name> # Switch output device
mac audio status              # Show audio settings
```

**Examples:**
```bash
mac audio volume 50           # Set volume to 50%
mac audio volume up           # Increase by configured step (default: 10)
mac audio devices --json      # List devices as JSON
mac audio output-device "External Speakers"  # Switch output
```

---

### 📸 screenshot — Screenshot Settings & Management

Screenshot location, format, shadow, cursor settings.

```bash
mac screenshot location       # Show save location
mac screenshot location <path> # Set save location
mac screenshot format         # Show current format
mac screenshot format <png|jpg|tiff|gif>  # Set format
mac screenshot shadow on      # Include window shadow
mac screenshot shadow off     # Exclude window shadow
mac screenshot shadow toggle  # Toggle shadow
mac screenshot cursor show    # Show cursor in screenshots
mac screenshot cursor hide    # Hide cursor in screenshots
mac screenshot cursor toggle  # Toggle cursor visibility
mac screenshot naming sequential # Sequential naming (Screenshot 1, 2...)
mac screenshot naming timestamp # Timestamp naming
mac screenshot status         # Show all screenshot settings
mac screenshot reset          # Reset to defaults
```

**Examples:**
```bash
mac screenshot location ~/Pictures/Screenshots  # Custom save path
mac screenshot format jpg     # Use JPEG format
mac screenshot shadow off     # No shadow on window screenshots
mac screenshot status         # See all settings
```

---

## Configuration

SuperMac stores configuration in `~/.supermac/config.yaml`.

```yaml
version: 1

output:
  color: true
  format: text         # text | json | quiet

updates:
  check: true
  channel: stable      # stable | beta

modules:
  screenshot:
    location: Desktop
    format: PNG
    shadow: false
  audio:
    volume_step: 10
  display:
    brightness_step: 10

aliases:
  kp: "dev kill-port"
  dark: "display dark-mode"
  light: "display light-mode"
  ip: "network ip"
  cleanup: "system cleanup"
```

### Config Commands

```bash
mac config list               # Show all settings
mac config get output.format  # Get specific value
mac config set output.format json  # Set value
mac config edit               # Open in $EDITOR
```

---

## Shell Completions

```bash
# Zsh
mac completion zsh > ~/.zfunc/_mac

# Bash
mac completion bash > /etc/bash_completion.d/mac

# Fish
mac completion fish > ~/.config/fish/completions/mac.fish
```

---

## JSON Output

Every command supports `--json` for scripting:

```bash
mac system memory --json
mac wifi status --json
mac audio devices --json
mac network ip --json
```

Example output:
```json
{
  "type": "success",
  "message": "Memory: 16 GB total, 8 GB free"
}
```

---

## Project

- **License**: MIT
- **Author**: CosmoLabs
- **Repository**: https://github.com/cosmolabs-org/supermac
- **Website**: https://cosmolabs.org

content.text: Screenshot module has vet warnings treated as build failures. These are `Info(msg)` calls where `msg` contains `%` from user input. Quick fix — use `fmt.Sprintf` properly:

content.content: No matches found

content.text: The issue is passing dynamic strings to `Info(msg, args...)` — the `vet` tool sees them as format strings. Fix by wrapping in `%s`:

input.old_string: ctx.Output.Info(loc)
input.new_string: ctx.Output.Info("%s", loc)

content.text:
# Continuation Prompt - Session Handoff

Generate a continuation prompt so the next session picks up exactly where this one left off.

## Usage

```bash
/continuation-prompt                              # Auto-analyze session, save prompt
/continuation-prompt "also cover auth refactor"   # Add extra context
/continuation-prompt --quick                      # Display only, no file
/continuation-prompt --status                     # Check pending prompts
```

## Zero-Arg Default Behavior

When invoked without arguments, **do not ask what to include**. You already have full session context. Automatically:

1. **Scan session state**: TaskList, completed work, pending tasks, git log since session start, files modified, decisions made
2. **Decide create vs update**: If a pending prompt from TODAY exists for this branch → update it. Otherwise → create new.
3. **Write the prompt** with everything needed to continue — no user input required.

The user running `/continuation-prompt` with no args means: *"Package up everything I didn't finish so I can resume later."*

## When to Use

| Situation | Command |
|-----------|---------|
| Quick handoff, paste now | `/handoff` |
| Documented handoff, save for later | `/continuation-prompt` (this) |
| End of session (runs this automatically) | `/session-end` |

## Execution

### 1. Check Flags
- `--status`, `--check` → status mode (run `ccs session-status --prompts`, display verbatim)
- `--quick`, `--nofile` → display mode (no file saved)
- `--force` → skip existing prompt check
- Otherwise → file mode (save to docs/prompts/)

### 2. Analyze Current Session (automatic — no user input needed)

**Think holistically, not mechanically.** The continuation prompt is a narrative briefing for your future self — not a checkbox dump. It should convey:

- **Where we stand**: Project momentum, what phase we're in, what's working well
- **What got done this session**: The story of the session, not just a git log
- **Where we're headed next**: Strategic direction, not just pending tasks
- **What didn't get finished and why**: Context matters — was it deprioritized, blocked, or just ran out of time?

Extract from your conversation context:

| Source | What to Extract |
|--------|----------------|
| **Session narrative** | The arc of the session — what we set out to do, what happened, where we ended up |
| **TaskList** | Pending/in-progress tasks = next session's goals |
| **Completed tasks** | Summary of what got done (context for next session) |
| **Git log** | `git log --oneline` since session start — what was committed |
| **Uncommitted changes** | `git status --short` — anything in-progress |
| **Continuation prompts loaded** | If `/run-continuation` was used, reference the source prompt and note which goals were completed vs carried forward |
| **Decisions made** | Key choices that affect future work |
| **Blocked items** | Anything that couldn't be completed and why |
| **Project trajectory** | Roadmap items in flight, feedback patterns, what the next logical milestone is |

### 3. Check for Existing Prompts (File Mode Only)

Skip if `--force`.

**Step 3a — Check pending prompts**:
1. Run `ccs prompts pending --recent 14 --brief`
2. If a pending prompt from TODAY on THIS branch exists → **update it** (add new goals, mark completed ones)
3. If pending prompts exist but for different scope → create new, reference them as carry-overs
4. If no pending prompts → create new

**Only ask the user if genuinely ambiguous** (e.g., two pending prompts both partially relevant). For the common case — just act.

**Step 3b — Carry-Over Detection**:
1. Run `ccs prompts pending --recent 30 --brief`
2. Filter for incomplete goals (`goals_completed < goals_total`)
3. Exclude ABANDONED prompts
4. Reference (don't duplicate) in carry-over section

### 4. Generate Prompt

**The Context section sets the vibe. The Goals section drives the work. The "Where We're Headed" section provides strategic gravity.**

Derive goals from:
1. **Pending TaskList items** → direct conversion to goals
2. **Loaded continuation prompt goals** that weren't completed → carry forward
3. **User-provided context** (if any args were passed) → additional goals
4. **Observed unfinished patterns** → things you started but didn't complete
5. **Strategic next steps** → what logically follows from this session's work (even if not explicitly tasked)

**MANDATORY: Carry-Over Tasks.** Before generating goals, check for incomplete tasks in the current session's TaskList. Any tasks with status `in_progress` or `pending` MUST be included in the prompt as a dedicated `## Carry-Over Tasks` section (see template below). Format each as:
```
- [ ] {task description} (was: {status})
```
This ensures no in-flight work is lost between sessions. If session-end Phase 2.5 flagged incomplete tasks, use that list directly.

Each goal should be specific and actionable — not vague. Include file paths, issue IDs, and concrete next steps.

**MANDATORY**: Always include a `## File Scope` section (see template below) listing `files_modified` and `files_created`. Populate from `git diff master...HEAD --name-only`, planning docs, and goal-referenced files. This is required for `/orchestra dispatch` conflict graph generation.

**Context section guidance**: Write it like you're briefing someone returning after a weekend. They need to feel the project's pulse — not just read a changelog. What's the energy? What's working? What's the next big unlock?

### 5. Output

**File mode** (default):
- Path: `ccs prompts path --slug {slug}` (auto-detects worktree)
- Save prompt with YAML frontmatter
- Validate: `ccs -q prompts check {path}`
- Display: `✅ Continuation prompt saved → {path}`

**Display mode** (`--quick`):
- Output full prompt to terminal
- Display: "Copy and paste into a new session"

## Prompt Template

```yaml
---
title: "{descriptive title}"
created: {YYYY-MM-DD}
status: PENDING
priority: {critical|high|medium|low}
branch: {branch-name}
origin: "/continuation-prompt"
tags: [continuation]
goals_total: {count}
goals_completed: 0
carried_over_from: null
carried_over_goals: 0
related_prompts: []
---
```

## Prompt Body Template

```markdown
# {Title} - Continuation Prompt

## File Scope
```yaml
files_modified:
  - path/to/modified/file1.go
  - path/to/modified/file2.go
files_created:
  - path/to/new/file1.go
```

**MANDATORY for all continuation prompts.** Populate from:
1. Planning doc's implementation steps (if linked)
2. Git diff of current session's changes: `git diff master...HEAD --name-only`
3. Files explicitly mentioned in goals

This section enables `/orchestra dispatch` to build conflict graphs for parallel worktree management without parsing free-form text.

## Context

{The narrative. Not just "project X on branch Y" — convey the feel:
Where are we in the project's lifecycle? What phase? What's the momentum like?
What did this session accomplish and how does it move the needle?
2-4 sentences that orient the next session immediately.}

## What Got Done

{Session accomplishments as a cohesive story. Group related work.
Mention commits, issue IDs, and impact — not just file paths.}

## Goals

### [ ] 1. {Goal title}
{Specific instructions: file paths, issue IDs, what to do, acceptance criteria.}

### [ ] 2. {Goal title}
{Same level of detail.}

## Carry-Over Tasks
{Include if the ending session had incomplete TaskList items.
 These are tasks that were in_progress or pending when the session ended.}
- [ ] {task description} (was: in_progress)
- [ ] {task description} (was: pending)

## Carry-Overs
{Only if pending prompts with incomplete goals exist. Reference, don't duplicate:}
1. **[PRIORITY] prompt-title** (X/Y goals)
   → `docs/prompts/YYYY-MM-DD-prompt.md`

## Where We're Headed

{Strategic context: what milestone is next, what roadmap items are in play,
what patterns are emerging from feedback. Help the next session understand
not just WHAT to do but WHY it matters and what comes after.}

## Priority Order
1. {Most important / blocking other work}
2. {High value / quick wins}
3. {Nice to have / exploratory}
```

After creating, validate: `ccs -q prompts check {filename}`

## Related

`/run-continuation` • `/handoff` • `/session-end`
